title: Ensure that the --auto-tls argument is not set to true

input_row.title: Ensure that the --auto-tls argument is not set to true
input_row.description: Do not use self-signed certificates for TLS.
input_row.rationale: etcd is a highly-available key value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. These objects are sensitive in nature and should not be available to unauthenticated clients. You should enable the client authentication via valid certificates to secure the access to the etcd service.
input_row.audit: OpenShift configures etcd with secure communication. Openshift installs etcd as static pods on control plane nodes, and mounts the configuration files from /etc/etcd/ on the host. The etcd.conf file includes auto-tls configurations as referenced in /etc/etcd/etcd.conf . OpenShift 4 includes multiple CAs providing independent chains of trust, which ensure that a platform CA will never accidentally sign a certificate that can be used for the wrong purpose, increasing the security posture of the cluster. These internal self-signing CAs enable automation because the key is known to the cluster. The certificates generated by each CA are used to identify a particular OpenShift platform component to another OpenShift platform component. The OpenShift CAs are managed by the cluster and are only used within the cluster. • Each cluster CA can only issue certificates for its own purpose within its own cluster. • CAs for one OpenShift cluster cannot influence CAs for a different OpenShift cluster, thus avoiding cross-cluster interference. • Cluster CAs cannot be influenced by an external CA that the cluster does not control. Run the following command to verify if auto-TLS is enabled: for i in $(oc get pods -oname -n openshift-etcd) do oc exec -n openshift-etcd -c etcd $i -- \ ps -o command= -C etcd | grep -- '--auto-tls=true 2>&1>/dev/null' ; \ echo $? done Verify that 1 is returned for each etcd member.
input_row.remediation: This setting is managed by the cluster etcd operator. No remediation required.
input_row.impact: Clients will not be able to use self-signed certificates for TLS.
input_row.default_value: By default, OpenShift configures etcd to use a cluster CA which creates self-signed certificates. These internal self-signing CAs enable automation because the key is known to the cluster. The certificates generated by each CA are used to identify a particular OpenShift platform component to another OpenShift platform component. The OpenShift CAs are managed by the cluster and are only used within the cluster. • Each cluster CA can only issue certificates for its own purpose within its own cluster. • CAs for one OpenShift cluster cannot influence CAs for a different OpenShift cluster, thus avoiding cross-cluster interference. • Cluster CAs cannot be influenced by an external CA that the cluster does not control. This configuration cannot be changed.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Check etcd Pods Configuration:**
   - Use IBM Cloud CLI to list etcd pods with the following command:
     ```
     oc get pods -n openshift-etcd
     ```
   - Record the names of the etcd pods returned.

2. **Inspect Command for Each Pod:**
   - For each etcd pod retrieved from the previous command, execute:
     ```
     oc exec -n openshift-etcd -c etcd <pod_name> -- ps -o command= -C etcd
     ```
   - Replace `<pod_name>` with the actual name of the pod.
   - Check if the command output contains `--auto-tls=true`.

3. **Verify Output:**
   - Confirm that the command returns “1” for each etcd member.
   - If any etcd member returns “0,” it indicates that the `--auto-tls` argument is set to true.

4. **Expected Secure Configuration:**
   - Ensure that none of the etcd instances are using `--auto-tls=true`. 
   - Compliance is maintained when all commands return “1.”

5. **Document Findings:**
   - Record any deviations found in the audit logs for follow-up remediation.

By following these steps, you can verify compliance with the control ensuring that the `--auto-tls` argument is not set to true, thus maintaining the security of etcd communication.
